The director needs to arrange the scenes so that no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade. The release years are 1994, 2004, 2007, 2012, and 2015 which correspond to the decades {1990-1999}, {2000-2009}, {2000-2009}, {2010-2019}, and {2010-2019} respectively. The shooting days required for the scenes are 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 days. To minimize the total shooting days while following the constraint, we can arrange the scenes in the order 1994 (4 days), 2012 (10 days), 2004 (6 days), 2015 (12 days), 2007 (8 days). This sequence ensures no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade and the total shooting days are minimized to 4+10+6+12+8=40 days.  
******  
The director has a budget of ₹50 lakhs and the cost per day for each scene is ₹1, ₹1.5, ₹2, ₹2.5, and ₹3 lakhs respectively. To maximize the number of completed scenes without exceeding the budget, we can formulate a linear programming problem where the objective is to maximize the number of scenes completed while ensuring the total cost does not exceed ₹50 lakhs. One possible solution is to shoot the scenes with the lowest costs first, completing 4 scenes (costing 1+1.5+2+2.5=7 lakhs) and leaving 43 lakhs for additional scenes. Another solution is to shoot the scenes with the highest costs first, completing 3 scenes (costing 2+2.5+3=7.5 lakhs) and leaving 42.5 lakhs for additional scenes. The maximum number of scenes that can be completed within the budget is 4 in the first case and 3 in the second case.
